name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ أَشِدَّآءُ عَلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَآءُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ تَرَىٰهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۖ سِيمَاهُمْ فِى وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلسُّجُودِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۚ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلْإِنجِيلِ كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْـَٔهُۥ فَـَٔازَرَهُۥ فَٱسْتَغْلَظَ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَىٰ سُوقِهِۦ يُعْجِبُ ٱلزُّرَّاعَ لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ ۗ وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًۢا (٢٩)
```

```english
Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace, and [His] pleasure. Their mark is [visible] on their faces, from the effect of prostration. Such is their description in the Torah and their description in the Evangel. Like a tillage that sends out its shoots and builds them up, and they grow stout and settle on their stalks, impressing the sowers, so that He may enrage the faithless by them. Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. (29)
```

## (Commentary)

This verse is the conclusion of the Surah. It describes the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those with him using the same descriptions found in the Torah and the Gospel, and it promises those among them who believe and perform righteous deeds a beautiful promise. The verse is connected to what preceded it, where He informed that He sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth.

His saying, the Exalted: **مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ** — **Muhammad is the Apostle of Allah** (48:29) until the end of the verse. It is apparent that this is a subject and a predicate, forming a complete sentence. It has also been said that **مُحَمَّدٌ** (Muhammad) is the predicate of an omitted subject, which is a pronoun referring back to "the Messenger" in the previous verse, the implied meaning being: "He is Muhammad." In this case, **رَسُولُ اللهِ** (the Apostle of Allah) would be an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان), an adjective (صفة), or a substitute (بدل). It has also been suggested that **مُحَمَّدٌ** is the subject, while **رَسُولُ اللهِ** is an explanatory apposition, an adjective, or a substitute, and **الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ** (those who are with him) is conjoined to the subject, with **أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ** (hard against the faithless) etc., being the predicate of the subject.

His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ** — **and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves** (48:29) is a subject and a predicate. The speech is directed toward describing those who are with him, and the mentioned hardness and mercy are among their attributes.

The following of the phrase **أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ** (hard against the faithless) with the phrase **رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ** (merciful amongst themselves) is to dispel any potential illusion that their being hard against the disbelievers might necessitate some hardness among themselves. Thus, He dispelled this with His saying: **رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ** (merciful amongst themselves). The two sentences together indicate that their conduct toward the disbelievers is one of hardness, while their conduct among the believers is one of mercy.

His saying: **تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً** — **You see them bowing and prostrating** (48:29). The terms **الركع** (those bowing) and **السجد** (those prostrating) are the plurals of **راكع** (one who bows) and **ساجد** (one who prostrates). What is meant by their being "bowing and prostrating" is their performance of prayer (الصلاة). The verb **تَراهُمْ** (you see them) implies continuity (الاستمرار). The sum total is that they are constant in prayer. This sentence serves as a subsequent predicate for "those who are with him."

His saying: **يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْواناً** — **seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure** (48:29). Seeking (الابتغاء) means searching or requesting. Grace (الفضل) refers to a gift (العطية), which is the reward (الثواب). Pleasure (الرضوان) is more intensive than mere satisfaction (الرضا).

If this sentence is intended to explain their objective in bowing and prostrating, it would be most appropriate for it to be a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the object pronoun in **تَراهُمْ** (you see them). However, if it is intended to explain their objective in life in general—as is apparent—then it is another predicate for "those who are with him."

His saying: **سِيماهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ مِنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ** — **Their mark is on their faces, from the effect of prostration** (48:29). The mark (السيما) means the sign (العلامة). The phrase **سِيماهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ** (their mark is on their faces) is a subject and a predicate, and **مِنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ** (from the effect of prostration) is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the latent pronoun in the predicate, or an explanation of the mark. That is, their prostration to Allah in humility and submissiveness has left an effect on their faces—an effect which is the mark of humility (الخشوع) before Allah, by which anyone who sees them recognizes them. Close to this meaning is what is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) that it refers to staying awake for prayer (السهر في الصلاة).

It has also been said that it refers to the trace of dust on their foreheads because they used to prostrate only on the earth, not on garments.

It has further been said that it refers to their mark on the Day of Resurrection, such that the places of their prostration will then be radiant and luminous.

His saying: **ذلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنْجِيلِ** — **That is their description in the Torah, and their description in the Evangel** (48:29). The description (المثل) here means the attribute (الصفة). That is, what We have described them with—that they are hard against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves, etc.—is their description that We have provided in the two books, the Torah and the Gospel.

Thus, His saying **وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنْجِيلِ** (and their description in the Evangel) is conjoined to His saying **مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ** (their description in the Torah). However, it has been said that the phrase **وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنْجِيلِ** (and their description in the Evangel) etc., is a new sentence (استئناف) disconnected from what preceded it. In this case, it would be a subject whose predicate is His saying: **كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْأَهُ** (like a tillage that sends out its shoots) etc. According to this view, their description in the Torah is that they are hard against the disbelievers—up to His saying—"from the effect of prostration," while their description in the Gospel is that they are like a tillage that sends out its shoots, etc.

His saying: **كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْأَهُ فَآزَرَهُ فَاسْتَغْلَظَ فَاسْتَوى عَلى سُوقِهِ يُعْجِبُ الزُّرَّاعَ** — **like a tillage that sends out its shoots and builds them up, and they grow stout and settle on their stems, delighting the sowers** (48:29). The shoot of a plant (شطء النبات) refers to its offshoots that are produced from it and grow around it. Building up (الإيزار) means assistance (الإعانة). Growing stout (الاستغلاظ) means becoming thick. Stems (السوق) is the plural of stem (ساق). Sowers (الزراع) is the plural of sower (زارع).

The meaning is: they are like a tillage that produced its offshoots, which then assisted it, so it became strong and thick, standing upright on its stems, delighting the sowers with the quality of its growth.

In this, there is an allusion to the believers' continuous increase in number, preparation, and strength day by day. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: **لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ الْكُفَّارَ** — **that He may enrage the faithless through them** (48:29).

His saying: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً** — **Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward** (48:29). The pronoun in **مِنْهُمْ** (of them) refers to "those who are with him," and the preposition **مِنَ** (of/from) denotes partitivity (التبعيض), as is the apparent and immediate meaning understood from such a structure. This speech implies that the promise of forgiveness and a great reward is conditional upon faith (الإيمان)—both in its inception and its continuation—and the performance of righteous deeds (عمل الصالحات). Therefore, if there were among them those who never believed at all, such as the hypocrites who were not recognized for their hypocrisy—as alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْ أَهْلِ الْمَدِينَةِ مَدَرُوا عَلَى النِّفاقِ لا تَعْلَمُهُمْ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ** — **and [some] of the people of Madinah [who] are habituated to hypocrisy. You do not know them; We know them** (9:101)—or those who believed initially and then associated partners with Allah and disbelieved—as in His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ ارْتَدُّوا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَى** — **Indeed those who turned back after guidance had become manifest to them** (47:25) until He said: **وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَأَرَيْناكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ** — **And if We wished, We would show them to you, and you would recognize them by their mark** (47:30)—or those who believed but did not perform righteous deeds—as can be inferred from the verses of the Slander (آيات الإفك) and the verse of verification regarding the report of the transgressor (الفاسق) and similar instances—then such individuals are not included in the promise of forgiveness and a great reward.

A parallel to this conditionality is what preceded in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبايِعُونَكَ إِنَّما يُبايِعُونَ اللهَ يَدُ اللهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَمَنْ نَكَثَ فَإِنَّما يَنْكُثُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَوْفى بِما عاهَدَ عَلَيْهُ اللهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْراً عَظِيماً** — **Indeed those who swear allegiance to you, swear allegiance only to Allah: the hand of Allah is above their hands. Then whosoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own soul’s detriment, and whoever fulfills what he has pledged to Allah, He will give him a great reward** (48:10). This is also supported by what Ibn Abbas understood from His saying, the Exalted: **فَعَلِمَ ما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ** — **He knew what was in their hearts, and He sent down composure on them** (48:18), where he interpreted it by saying: "Composure (السكينة) was only sent down upon those from whom He knew there would be fulfillment." This narration has been mentioned previously.

Another parallel to this verse regarding conditionality is His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — **Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth** (24:55) until He said: **وَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ** — **and whoever is faithless after that—it is they who are the transgressors** (24:55).

It has been said that **مِنَ** (of/from) in the verse is explanatory (بيانية) rather than partitive (تبعيضية), which would imply that the promise includes all those who were with him.

This is refuted—as has been noted—by the fact that the explanatory **من** does not enter upon a pronoun at all in the Arabic language. The citation of His saying, the Exalted: **لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا لَعَذَّبْنَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ** — **had they been separate, We would have surely punished the faithless among them** (48:25) as evidence for this is based on returning the pronoun in **تَزَيَّلُوا** (they been separate) to the believers and the pronoun in **مِنْهُمْ** (among them) to the disbelievers. However, it has already been explained in the commentary of that verse that both pronouns refer to the collective group of believers and disbelievers among the people of Mecca; thus, **مَنْ** is partitive, not explanatory.

Beyond all this, if the promise of forgiveness, or forgiveness itself, included them absolutely without the condition of faith and righteous deeds—such that they were forgiven whether they believed or associated partners, and whether they acted righteously or transgressed—it would clearly follow that all religious obligations (التكاليف الدينية) would be futile and lifted from them. This is something rejected by both the Book and the Sunnah. Thus, this conditionality is established in itself even if it were not explicitly mentioned in the wording. Indeed, the Exalted said regarding His prophets: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — **and had they ascribed partners [to Him], what they used to do would have surely come to naught** (6:88). He established this for His prophets, who are infallible (معصومون); how then could it not apply to those below them?

If it is said: The conditionality of the promise of forgiveness and a great reward upon faith and righteous deeds is a rational condition (اشتراط عقلي), as mentioned, and there is no way to deny it. However, the context of His saying: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ** (Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds) testifies to their being characterized by faith and righteous deeds, and that they possess the condition.

This is especially so considering the placement of **مِنْهُمْ** (of them) after the phrase **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ** (those who have faith and do righteous deeds), which indicates that righteous deeds are inseparable from them, unlike His saying in the verse of Light: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ** — **Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors** (24:55), as some have noted. This is also supported by His saying in their praise: **تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْواناً** (You see them bowing and prostrating, seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure), which indicates continuity.

We reply: As for the delayed placement of **مِنْهُمْ** (of them) in the verse, it is not to indicate that righteous deeds are inseparable from them. Rather, it is because the subject of the ruling is the collective group of "those who believe and perform righteous deeds," and the effect of forgiveness and reward does not follow from mere faith without righteous deeds. Thus, His saying **مِنْهُمْ** (of them) relates to the entire subject, and it is proper for it to be mentioned after the completion of the subject, which is "those who believe and perform righteous deeds." As for the advancement of the pronoun in His saying: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ** (Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors), it is because it is framed as glad tidings (بشرى) for the believers, and it is more appropriate for glad tidings to hasten the address to those being given the news, so they may be energized and delighted to receive it.

As for the indication of continuity in His saying: **تَراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً** (You see them bowing and prostrating) etc., it only indicates continuity in the past up until the present moment. As for the future, it does not. The crux of the problem regarding the futility of religious obligations concerns the future, not the past. This is because the forgiveness of past sins does not conflict with the attachment of religious obligation; rather, it reinforces it. This is in contrast to the attachment of absolute forgiveness to what is yet to come, for that cannot coexist with the continued validity of masterly obligation (التكليف المولوي), which would thereby be lifted—a notion that is certainly false. Furthermore, the lifting of obligations would necessitate the lifting of disobedience (المعصية), and with its lifting, the very subject of forgiveness would be removed. Thus, the existence of such forgiveness would necessitate its own non-existence.
